title: Problem 6 (Scott)
statement_text: For $n$ even, what is the probability that $G_{n,1/2}$ can be partitioned into two sets, each inducing odd subgraphs?
context_text: Described by the authors as 'one of the starting points in the random setting.' It was previously known from [10] that the answer is at least $1/2 + o(1)$.
paper_contribution: The paper proves the answer is $2/3 + o(1)$ and, more precisely, determines the full asymptotic distributions of $X_n^{(2,0)}$, $X_n^{(1,1)}$, and $X_n^{(0,2)}$ explicitly (Theorem 7).
paper_title: Counting partitions of $G_{n,1/2}$ with degree congruence conditions

title: Question on $q$ parts (Ferber, Hardiman and Krivelevich)
statement_text: Does Theorem 3 still hold with $q$ parts instead of $q+1$ parts? That is, does almost every $G_{n,1/2}$ have a vertex partition into $q$ sets $A_1, \ldots, A_q$ such that in each induced subgraph $G[A_i]$ all degrees are $x \bmod q$?
context_text: Ferber, Hardiman and Krivelevich [4] proved that $q+1$ parts always suffice for any $q$ and $x$, and asked whether $q$ parts would also suffice. A first-moment argument already shows $q-1$ parts are insufficient.
paper_contribution: The paper provides a negative answer: Theorems 4 and 5 show that the number of good $q$-partitions converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with finite mean, implying that almost surely no such $q$-partition exists.
notes: Not given an explicit numbered label; identified from the phrase 'a natural question asked in [4]'.
paper_title: Counting partitions of $G_{n,1/2}$ with degree congruence conditions
